Top-level packages are purpose-named and reasonably scoped: grammar, indexer, query, tagging, api, config, tools, utils, corpus — eight packages for ~56 production files is appropriate fan-out.

indexer/hybrid/ contains 22 files flat (threshold: 10), mixing at least five distinct concern groups: core processing (HybridIndexer, CollocationsBuilder, SinglePassConlluProcessor, HybridConllUProcessor), data model records (Collocation, CollocationEntry, TermStatistics, SentenceDocument), codec/IO layer (LemmaIdsCodec, LemmaLexiconReader, LemmaLexiconWriter, PairRunIO, TokenSequenceCodec, IntArrayBuffer), statistics builders (StatisticsIndexBuilder, StatisticsReader, StatisticsConverter), and migration/utility helpers (LexiconFromStatsMigrator, LongIntHashMap, LongIntPairSort, LemmaIdAssigner, PositionalTokenStream). A developer looking for, say, 'where is the binary serialization?' must scan 22 files with no sub-package guidance.

query/ contains 15 files mixing predicate combinators (AndPredicate, OrPredicate, NotPredicate, LemmaPredicate, TagPredicate, PosGroupPredicate, WordPredicate — 7 small classes), infrastructure interfaces/models (QueryExecutor, QueryResults, Token, TokenWindow, TokenPredicate), executor implementations (BlackLabQueryExecutor, CQLVerifier), and the high-level semantic exploration component (SemanticFieldExplorer at 889 LOC). These concern layers have different change rates and ownership boundaries.

TestQuery.java lives at the repository root with package declaration absent, fan_in=0, fan_out=0. It contains a hardcoded Windows absolute path ('D:\\corpora_philsci\\bi') and is a scratch development driver that was not promoted into tools/ or removed. It imports from query.BlackLabQueryExecutor.

corpus/ has 2 files (CorpusBuilder, PostgresCorpusSampler) with avg_fan_in=0.0 and avg_fan_out=0.0 — nothing imports from this package and it has no test coverage. It is functionally orphaned and creates a misleading directory entry that implies corpus-building is a supported path.
WordSketchApiServer uses Builder pattern with explicit dependency injection; all collaborators (QueryExecutor, GrammarConfigLoader) arrive through the builder, not via globals.
Main.handleServerCommand() builds the object graph in a single explicit sequence: executor -> grammarConfig -> server; no hidden boot-order magic.

Static members across the codebase are pure constants (magic numbers, logger, regex Pattern literals) or immutable maps initialized in static blocks with no I/O — SimpleTagger's static VERB_FORMS block is data-only.

SemanticFieldExplorer provides a two-constructor design: one that opens a BlackLabIndex itself (SemanticFieldExplorer(String indexPath)), and one that accepts an injected executor for testing — but the API server always calls the self-opening constructor.

In WordSketchApiServer, three request handlers (/api/semantic-field/explore, /api/semantic-field/compare, and the explore-multi path) each call 'new SemanticFieldExplorer(indexPath)' directly, which opens a new BlackLab index connection mid-request instead of reusing the shared this.executor.

None of the three SemanticFieldExplorer usages in WordSketchApiServer are wrapped in try-with-resources; close() is called on the happy path only, so any exception between construction and close() leaks the index handle.

GrammarConfigLoader.createDefaultEnglish() hardcodes a relative path 'grammars/relations.json' — callers must run from the project root; this factory is never called in the main codebase but is a latent CWD-dependent init trap.

grammarConfig is initialized to null in Main.handleServerCommand() (graceful optional load); this propagates through 8+ null-guards in WordSketchApiServer request handlers rather than being resolved to a null-object or explicit Optional at startup.

WordSketchApiServer registers 10+ endpoint contexts (health, sketch, relations, semantic-field/explore, semantic-field/explore-multi, semantic-field, semantic-field/examples, concordance/examples, visual/radial, bcql) with no auth middleware or decorator on any of them — uniformly open, consistent with a local research tool.

sendJsonResponse() always sets Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * (line ~1209), but sendError() does not set any CORS header. Error responses from every endpoint therefore violate the trust boundary established for success responses.

POST endpoints (api/bcql, api/visual/radial) handle OPTIONS preflight manually and set CORS headers on 204/200 responses, but their error branches also fall through to sendError() which lacks CORS headers — the same inconsistency.

No authentication tokens, session checks, role constants, or permission literals exist anywhere in the codebase. The PostgresCorpusSampler credentials are passed as constructor parameters at build time, not accessible via the HTTP API.
authorization_consistency.issues_preventing_higher_score: sendError() does not set Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *, so cross-origin error responses are blocked by browsers while all success responses allow any origin. This is a consistent rule applied inconsistently: success paths declare an open trust boundary but error paths silently revoke it.

evidence: Package names (api, config, corpus, grammar, indexer, query, tagging, tools, utils, viz) are semantically plausible and domain-aligned on the surface.

Main.java exposes only three commands (blacklab-index, blacklab-query, server), but CLAUDE.md (the canonical dev guide) documents four different commands (index, conllu, query, server) — a new engineer following CLAUDE.md would immediately fail at the CLI.

indexer/ contains three parallel indexer subsystems: LuceneIndexer (root-level, old), indexer/blacklab/BlackLabConllUIndexer (active, used by Main), and indexer/hybrid/ (20+ classes: HybridIndexer, SinglePassConlluProcessor, CollocationsBuilder, etc.) — none of the hybrid classes are reachable from Main or WordSketchApiServer.

tagging/ConllUProcessor.java and tagging/CorpusProcessor.java import pl.marcinmilkowski.word_sketch.indexer.LuceneIndexer (the old indexer), not the active BlackLab backend. They compile as live code but are disconnected from the active pipeline.

20+ .bak and .tmp.* files are version-controlled inside production source packages: query/ has 10 such files (CQLToLuceneCompiler, HybridQueryExecutor, WordSketchQueryExecutor, ConcordanceExplorer, QueryExecutorFactory, SemanticFieldExplorer.bak, SnowballCollocations.bak, plus 5 .tmp variants), grammar/ has 3, indexer/hybrid/ has 2, tools/ has 2, viz/ has 4. This makes it impossible to navigate any of these packages by filename and know what is active.

TestQuery.java is tracked at the project root with a hardcoded Windows path (D:\corpora_philsci\bi) — it is neither in src/test/ nor documented as a dev tool; it creates confusion about the project root's purpose.

corpus/ package (CorpusBuilder.java, PostgresCorpusSampler.java) uses the old LuceneIndexer and a PostgreSQL backend. It is neither referenced from Main nor documented as active, but it compiles as a live package alongside production code.
57 production files vs 20 test files; core business classes SemanticFieldExplorer (889 lines), WordSketchApiServer (1253 lines), CollocationsBuilder (1139 lines) have zero dedicated tests
TestBCQLParser.java contains only a main() method — it is never executed as a JUnit test
DiagnosticsIntegrationTest.skipDiagnostic() always passes: the method unconditionally returns or calls assertTrue(true), providing false CI confidence

DependencySketchTest, BlackLabDummyTest, BlackLabBcqlConcordanceTest silently return (no failure, no @Disabled) when hardcoded Windows path D:\corpora_philsci\bi does not exist — they appear to pass in any non-Windows CI environment

BlackLabBcqlConcordanceTest and DependencySketchTest use raw Java assert statements (e.g. 'assert results != null') which are no-ops unless the JVM is launched with -ea; assertions are effectively disabled
LongIntHashMap (135-line custom open-addressing hash map used in core indexing) has no test coverage despite non-trivial rehash and addTo logic
No integration test covers the indexer→query→API pipeline end-to-end; SemanticFieldExplorer has an injection constructor designed for mocking, but no mock-based test exists

In indexer/hybrid, HybridConllUProcessor uses 'ConllU' suffix matching the codebase-wide pattern (BlackLabConllUIndexer, ConllUProcessor in tagging), but sibling SinglePassConlluProcessor in the same package uses 'Conllu' (lowercase 'u'), deviating from the established spelling.

tagging/ConllUProcessor and tagging/CorpusProcessor use SLF4J string concatenation (logger.info("Processing " + file)) while sibling CoNLL-U processors HybridConllUProcessor and SinglePassConlluProcessor use proper SLF4J placeholder style (logger.info("Processing {}", file)), creating a logging style island in the tagging package.
Package-level naming conventions (PascalCase classes, camelCase methods, UPPER_SNAKE constants) are applied uniformly across all 11 packages without drift.

Resource management protocol (implements Closeable) is applied consistently across hybrid indexer classes; minor Closeable vs AutoCloseable variation exists between packages but does not cause confusion.
API layer (WordSketchApiServer) consistently catches IOException + Exception per endpoint and returns HTTP 500 with message — a sound top-level boundary
I/O-heavy classes (HybridIndexer, CollocationsBuilder, StatisticsReader, HybridConllUProcessor) all declare 'throws IOException', making failures explicit to callers
query/ directory strategy mix: returns_null=17, try_catch=9, throws=7, result_type=6 — three competing strategies in the same module

CorpusBuilder.buildIndex() line 117-119: 'catch (Exception e) { // Skip problematic sentences }' — completely silent, no logging, no counter; any NullPointerException, IndexOutOfBoundsException, or tagging failure is invisible

BlackLabQueryExecutor.getTotalFrequency() line 530: 'catch (Exception e) { return 0L; }' — silently returns 0 when frequency lookup fails, corrupting logDice scores without any indication; other public methods in the same class throw IOException

HybridConllUProcessor.buildSentence() line 210-212: 'catch (NumberFormatException e) { // Skip invalid token IDs }' — completely silent; ConllUProcessor.processCoNLLUSentence() handles the same case with logger.debug(), creating inconsistency between twin classes

HybridIndexer.writeStatistics() catches Exception twice (lines 242 and 257) and logs.warn() for binary stats and lexicon write failures — these files are required for query correctness but failures are demoted to warnings with no propagation

StatisticsConverter.convert() wraps IOException as RuntimeException inside the streaming lambda (line 113) — consistent with Java lambda constraints but differs from the throws IOException pattern used everywhere else in the same class
Predicate hierarchy (LemmaPredicate, TagPredicate, PosGroupPredicate, WordPredicate, AndPredicate, OrPredicate, NotPredicate) uses a perfectly uniform naming convention

HybridConllUProcessor and SinglePassConlluProcessor live in the same package (indexer.hybrid), both delegate to HybridIndexer, but are named along different axes: 'Hybrid' describes the output type while 'SinglePass' describes the algorithm
CoNLL-U is spelled three different ways across class names: ConllU (HybridConllUProcessor, ConllUProcessor) and Conllu (SinglePassConlluProcessor)
ConllUProcessor.processCoNLLUSentence() uses the generic 'process' verb; the method body calls indexer.addWord() for each token — it is indexing, not processing in any abstract sense

BlackLabQueryExecutor has six private methods all prefixed extractCollocateFrom*: extractCollocateFromXmlByPosition, extractCollocateFromMatchText, extractCollocateFromPattern, extractCollocateFromSnippet, extractCollocateFromMatch, extractCollocateFromPlainText — the repeated prefix obscures the significant difference in input format

StatisticsConverter and StatisticsIndexBuilder both deal with statistics persistence but the distinction between 'Converter' (TSV->binary) and 'IndexBuilder' (in-memory -> binary) is not immediately obvious from the names

QueryExecutor interface (query/QueryExecutor.java) has only one current runtime implementation (BlackLabQueryExecutor), but the abstraction is justified by explicit multi-implementation intent and its use of default methods for backward compatibility.

WordSketchApiServer.java casts the QueryExecutor field to BlackLabQueryExecutor in 5 places (lines 306, 350, 403, 455, 694) to call executeSurfacePattern(), which is not declared in the interface — callers consistently bypass the intended abstraction.
SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPredicates() (lines 262-270) is a pure forwarding wrapper: it passes all 5 parameters unchanged to explore() with no added behavior, policy, or translation.

SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern() (lines 87-97) accepts 10 parameters — seed, topPredicates, nounsPerPredicate, minShared, minLogDice, bcqlPattern, simplePattern, relationName, headPos, collocatePos — a wide parameter bag that hides domain boundaries.

CollocationsBuilder.buildRelationCollocations() (lines 216-303) is publicly callable but always produces empty results; the implementation immediately sets simpleRelations = Collections.emptyList() with a comment admitting the feature is DISABLED.

GrammarConfigLoader.RelationConfig has extractXposPattern() (line 528) and extractPosPattern() (line 536), each a one-line wrapper delegating to extractConstraintAttribute() with only the attribute name changed — no behavior, no translation.

LemmaLexiconReader.java (131 lines) is a tight, zero-waste abstraction: parallel arrays for O(1) ID lookup, buffer.duplicate() for safe concurrent reads, no pass-through wrappers — every method adds genuine value.
HybridIndexer.StatisticsCollector (inner class, lines 301-525) cleanly separates statistics collection concern from document indexing, is thread-safe via ConcurrentHashMap, and earns its abstraction.

GrammarConfigLoader.RelationConfig provides getFullPattern(), collocatePosGroup(), and getDeprel() — pattern manipulation utilities that meaningfully transform config data, justifying the abstraction.

issues.summary: executeSurfacePattern bypasses QueryExecutor interface via repeated downcasts in api and query layers
evidence: WordSketchApiServer.java lines 306, 350, 403, 455, 693-694: ((BlackLabQueryExecutor) executor).executeSurfacePattern(...) — all five main sketch handler paths cast through the interface

issues.summary: SemanticFieldExplorer primary constructor directly instantiates BlackLabQueryExecutor, bypassing injection
